commits.author: Tian, Feng
commits.message:
Add sycl implementation of memory_kernels for Intel XPU (#2902)

* Add SYCL/XPU port of mem_kernels with Intel optimizations and build scripts

Performance optimization for flash_infer on Intel XPU:

1. Added page_buffer_base_offset() and key_value_base_offset() helpers
   that compute loop-invariant base offsets (incl. integer division for
   flash_infer block mapping) once before the inner loop.

2. Added submit_multi_layer_kernel_fused_kv() that processes both K and V
   in a single work-group for non-MLA formats, halving work-group count
   and avoiding redundant slot lookups, pointer reads, and division.

3. Updated dispatch logic to use fused kernel for non-MLA formats
   (flash_infer, flash_attention, cross-layer) and original kernel for
   MLA formats.

4. Applied base-offset hoisting to unilateral kernel as well.

Signed-off-by: Feng Tian <feng.tian@intel.com>

commits.author: Ilya Yanok
commits.message:
nixl_storage: naive support for files + dynamic (#2936)

* nixl_storage: naive support for files + dynamic

static is not actually very usable with files: we bump into the OS
limits on the open files very quickly, limiting the size of the cache.
With tons of VRAM, having G3 of comparable size is not helping much, we
really need it to be much bigger.

This commit adds support for files in dynamic mode of nixl storage.
There are some naive things done:
1. No support for sharing the cache storage. We assume the worker has
   exclusive access to the files and it is always safe to overwrite
   existing files. Note that with TP!=1 we will have several workers
   with the same target directory, that's why it's important to have a
   worker id as part of the key, so they don't affect each other.
2. No eviction support. As before, dynamic mode has no evicting support.
   This kinda made sense when it was only working with OBJ storages, but
   now that is something to be aware of. It's not hard to add eviction
   though.
3. Flat directory with all the cache files. There are going to be a lot
   of them, especially provided we don't have eviction. Most filesystems
   don't optimize for the case of a directory with millions of files,
   and we constantly open/close files there, that might be a source
   of additional latency.
   - There is an existing PR to support sharding the directory, we can
     merge it as a band aid.
   - As a more advanced solution, we can do multi-layer subdirectory
     structure, like gds backend does.
4. We switched directly from having _all_ files open at once, to
   open/close _every_ single file on access. We might explore having an
   LRU cache of open files instead.

commits.author: longguo
commits.message:
fix(docker): install CLI requirements in standalone image so `lmcache server` boots (fixes #3353) (#3355)

fix(docker): install CLI requirements in standalone image so `lmcache server` boots

`lmcache/standalone` (v0.4.5, latest, nightly) crashes on
`lmcache server` with:

    ModuleNotFoundError: No module named 'openai'

Root cause: `setup.py` only puts `requirements/common.txt` and
`requirements/cuda{12,13}_core.txt` into `install_requires`.
`requirements/cli.txt` -- which contains `openai` (used by the
`bench` engine client) -- is not installed.

`lmcache server` is registered via `lmcache.cli.main:main`, which
does `from lmcache.cli.commands import ALL_COMMANDS`. That triggers
`lmcache.cli.commands._discover_commands()`, which uses
`discover_subclasses(...)` to **eagerly importlib.import_module**
every submodule under `cli/commands/`. That includes
`bench/__init__.py`, whose top-level imports chain into
`engine_bench/request_sender.py:11 -> from openai import AsyncOpenAI`,
and the standalone image has no `openai`.

`cli/commands/__init__.py:18-30` deliberately re-raises import
errors ("a broken CLI command module should fail loudly rather than
silently disappear from the CLI"), so the discovery cannot be
softened on the slim install path -- the install path itself has
to be fixed.

The other Docker images (`Dockerfile`, `Dockerfile.rocm`,
`Dockerfile.lightweight`, `Dockerfile.rocm-lightweight`) all
install `vllm[runai,tensorizer,...]` or are `FROM vllm/vllm-openai*`,
and `openai` arrives transitively through vLLM there. Only the
standalone image is vLLM-free and hits this crash.

Minimal fix: install `requirements/cli.txt` alongside the existing
`ray nvidia-ml-py` step in the base stage, using a
`--mount=type=bind` so the file does not persist into the image
(matching the `build.txt` style used later by the lmcache-build
stage).

Out of scope (intentional, can be a follow-up): the deeper question
of whether `cli.txt` should be in `install_requires`, an
`extras_require["cli"]` block in `setup.py`, or whether the
fail-loudly contract in `cli/commands/__init__.py` should be
loosened, is an architectural call I'd defer to maintainers.
Either of those would also fix #3353 (and would fix the same crash
for users who `pip install lmcache` outside of Docker), but
keeping this PR scoped to the reported failure mode -- the standalone
Docker image -- keeps the review surface tight and unblocks the
broken images today.

Fixes #3353

commits.author: Guy Ealey Morag
commits.message:
nixl_storage: Fix tests and remove from ignore list (#3200)

* nixl_storage: naive support for files + dynamic

static is not actually very usable with files: we bump into the OS
limits on the open files very quickly, limiting the size of the cache.
With tons of VRAM, having G3 of comparable size is not helping much, we
really need it to be much bigger.

This commit adds support for files in dynamic mode of nixl storage.
There are some naive things done:
1. No support for sharing the cache storage. We assume the worker has
   exclusive access to the files and it is always safe to overwrite
   existing files. Note that with TP!=1 we will have several workers
   with the same target directory, that's why it's important to have a
   worker id as part of the key, so they don't affect each other.
2. No eviction support. As before, dynamic mode has no evicting support.
   This kinda made sense when it was only working with OBJ storages, but
   now that is something to be aware of. It's not hard to add eviction
   though.
3. Flat directory with all the cache files. There are going to be a lot
   of them, especially provided we don't have eviction. Most filesystems
   don't optimize for the case of a directory with millions of files,
   and we constantly open/close files there, that might be a source
   of additional latency.
   - There is an existing PR to support sharding the directory, we can
     merge it as a band aid.
   - As a more advanced solution, we can do multi-layer subdirectory
     structure, like gds backend does.
4. We switched directly from having _all_ files open at once, to
   open/close _every_ single file on access. We might explore having an
   LRU cache of open files instead.

commits.author: GentleCold
commits.message:
[BugFix] fix sha256_cbor + async_loading type mismatch in _hash_tokens (#2999)

* [BugFix] fix sha256_cbor + async_loading type mismatch in _hash_tokens

When LMCACHE_PRE_CACHING_HASH_ALGORITHM=sha256_cbor is used together with
LMCACHE_ENABLE_ASYNC_LOADING=True, the async lookup path fails because
sha256_cbor (vLLM PR#23673) returns a 32-byte bytes digest. This digest is
placed into LookupRequestMsg.hashes which msgpack cannot encode as int,
raising OverflowError.

Fix: after calling hash_func in _hash_tokens, if the result is bytes,
fold the first 8 bytes into a uint64 int. This keeps all downstream code
(CacheEngineKey, msgpack serialisation) working with plain ints regardless
of the configured hash algorithm. Both the store path and the async lookup
path go through _hash_tokens, so the keys produced are always consistent.

Fixes #2997

Signed-off-by: GentleCold <gentlecold@qq.com>

* [BugFix] address review: fix NONE_HASH type and use public test interface

- token_database.py: convert NONE_HASH from bytes to int immediately after
  it is set from vLLM. This ensures the entire prefix-hash chain uses a
  consistent int type as canon_prefix input to hash_func, not bytes on the
  first chunk and int on subsequent ones.

- test_token_database.py: replace direct call to private _hash_tokens with
  the public process_tokens interface (make_key=False). Drop cbor2 import
  in favour of a simpler hashlib stub.

Signed-off-by: GentleCold <gentlecold@qq.com>

* [BugFix] normalize byte hash outputs in token database

- token_database.py: add a module-level helper to normalize hash outputs from vLLM hash functions into LMCache's int chunk-hash representation.

- Reuse the helper for both vLLM NONE_HASH initialization and token chunk hashing so sha256_cbor byte digests are folded consistently before entering the prefix hash chain.

commits.author: kumaneko
commits.message:
[Perf] Avoid redundant PCIe transfer on leader rank during retrieve (#3413)

* [Perf] Avoid redundant PCIe transfer on leader rank during retrieve

When `save_only_first_rank` is enabled (MLA path), the leader rank's
memory_objs returned from `_process_tokens_internal()` reference
CPU-resident L1 buffers. The broadcast step uploads each chunk to GPU
once via `raw_tensor.to(GPU)` so NCCL has a GPU send buffer, but the
result is discarded. The subsequent `batched_to_gpu` then reads the
same L1 bytes again over PCIe (via cudaHostGetDevicePointer zero-copy
mapping inside the multi_layer_kv_transfer kernel), making the leader
rank ~9 ms slower than passive ranks on every retrieve at our setup
(8192 tokens, tp=8, DeepSeek-R1-style MLA, H20 + PCIe Gen4).

This patch keeps the GPU-resident copy from the broadcast alive on the
leader and substitutes it as the source for `batched_to_gpu`, so the
kernel reads from HBM (~3 TB/s) instead of pinned host memory through
PCIe (~32 GB/s). The original CPU memory_objs remain in
`reordered_chunks` so the existing cleanup / ref-counting path is
unchanged. Passive ranks are unaffected (already pass GPU tensors).

Measured on tp=8 H20, DeepSeek-R1, 8192-token warm retrieve,
in-process connector:

| rank          | togpu_gpu before | after  |
|---------------|------------------|--------|
| 0  (leader)   | 9.42 ms          | 0.70 ms|
| 1-7 (passive) | 0.48 ms          | 0.48 ms|

End-to-end TTFT at c=1 improves by ~10 ms (~133 ms -> ~123 ms). All 8
ranks now finish `retrieve()` within ~0.2 ms of each other rather than
the leader trailing the others by ~9 ms.

Signed-off-by: kumaneko <crclq2018@gmail.com>

* [Perf] Move leader GPU substitute cleanup into try/finally

Address review feedback from gemini-code-assist on PR #3413: wrap the
batched_to_gpu call in try/finally so that the leader's
_leader_gpu_substitute_objs list is always cleared, even if
batched_to_gpu raises (e.g. CUDA OOM). Without this, a raise would
leave temporary GPU tensors held by the long-lived LMCacheEngine
until the next successful retrieve resets the list.

Signed-off-by: kumaneko <crclq2018@gmail.com>

* Fix mypy [has-type] error on _leader_gpu_substitute_objs

The previous patch declared self._leader_gpu_substitute_objs lazily
inside _broadcast_or_receive_memory_objs and accessed it via getattr/
hasattr in retrieve(). mypy could not infer the attribute's type
through that flow, failing the Code Quality CI check at line 871:

  Cannot determine type of "_leader_gpu_substitute_objs"  [has-type]

Per the LMCache coding standards ("Avoid Optional — initialize objects
even if empty"), declare the attribute in __init__ with its full type
annotation. The broadcast function now just resets it to a fresh list
at the start of the leader-side critical section, and the call site
reads it via plain attribute access (no getattr/hasattr guards).

Behavior is unchanged: non-leader ranks never populate the list, so
the substitution branch in retrieve() is gated by length-equality
against the chunk count, which is 0 on non-leaders and only matches
on the leader after the broadcast loop has populated it.

Verified with `pre-commit run --files lmcache/v1/cache_engine.py`:
all hooks (ruff, ruff-format, codespell, mypy, etc.) now pass.

Signed-off-by: kumaneko <crclq2018@gmail.com>

* Address review: skip list() on leader path; warn on substitute mismatch

Two cleanups from review feedback on PR #3413:

1. @maobaolong: hoist `memory_objs_for_togpu = list(memory_objs)` into
   the `else` branch so the leader-substitute path doesn't pay for an
   unnecessary `list()` allocation that gets immediately overwritten.

2. @chunxiaozheng: when on the leader rank with save_only_first_rank
   but the substitute list count does not match the memory_objs count,
   that indicates a bug or stale state (the broadcast should always
   have populated the substitute list to match). Log a warning and
   fall back to the CPU L1 source so retrieval is still correct but
   the anomaly is visible.

Also widen the static type of `_leader_gpu_substitute_objs` from
`List[TensorMemoryObj]` to `List[MemoryObj]` so mypy can see that
passing it to `batched_to_gpu(memory_objs: list[MemoryObj], ...)` is
type-safe (Python's `list` is invariant). The runtime objects are
still `TensorMemoryObj` instances built in `_broadcast_or_receive_
memory_objs`; only the static type widens.

Verified with `pre-commit run --files lmcache/v1/cache_engine.py`:
all hooks (ruff, ruff-format, mypy, codespell, isort, etc.) pass.

Signed-off-by: kumaneko <crclq2018@gmail.com>

commits.message:
feat(bench): server_bench supports --mode cpu and --transfer-mode (#3528)

* feat(bench): server_bench supports --mode cpu and --transfer-mode

- --mode cpu: allocate POSIX-SHM-backed KV cache tensors via
  CpuShmTensorWrapper; the server maps the same physical pages
  for zero-copy STORE/RETRIEVE.
- --transfer-mode {auto,handle,data}: explicit override of the
  routing. auto keeps the historical gpu->handle / cpu->data
  dispatch. data uses REGISTER_KV_CACHE_NON_GPU_CONTEXT +
  PREPARE/COMMIT two-phase path with client-side self-check.
- handle mode on CPU is left as TODO for a follow-up PR.
- docs: add Bench Server section to configuration.rst.

Signed-off-by: baoloongmao <baoloongmao@tencent.com>

* address review: merge cpu/gpu kv-cache allocator and fix chunk_size default

Per @hlin99 review on #3528: collapse _allocate_cpu_kv_cache and _allocate_gpu_kv_cache into a single _allocate_kv_cache(use_gpu=...). Most of the body was identical; the only divergence is whether to attach a CUDA device. Update command.py / tests to call the merged helper.

Also bump _send_store(chunk_size=...) default from 0 to 256 to match the typical server-side chunk size; the existing chunk_size > 0 guard is preserved by callers passing the runtime value explicitly.

Signed-off-by: baoloongmao <baoloongmao@tencent.com>

* address review: drop defensive getattr, return register tuple

Per @yoo-kumaneko on #3528:

- args.transfer_mode is always set by argparse, so drop the getattr() defensive fallback. A future rename will now fail loudly instead of silently falling back to 'auto'.

- _send_register_kv_cache used to return 'bool | RegisterNonGpuContextResponse' and the caller relied on the response being truthy to mean success. RegisterNonGpuContextResponse is a dataclass with no __bool__, so even an empty failure response would print OK. Switch to (success, Optional[response]) so the success flag is explicit, and treat an empty shm_name as failure.

Signed-off-by: baoloongmao <baoloongmao@tencent.com>

commits.author: Samuel Shen
commits.message:
[Chore][CI] Force vLLM Model Runner V1 in the PD comprehensive test (#3502)

[CI/CD] Force vLLM Model Runner V1 in the PD comprehensive test

The PD comprehensive test (k3-comprehensive-test) started timing out on
2026-06-02 when the floating vLLM nightly bumped from 0.22.1rc1.dev91 to
.dev95. That delta includes vllm-project/vllm#43458, which enables Model
Runner V2 (MRV2) by default for Llama/Mistral dense models -- including
meta-llama/Llama-3.2-1B-Instruct, the model used by pd.yaml.

MRV2 is incompatible with KV cache connectors, so the prefiller/decoder
(both driven through LMCacheConnectorV1) hang on the first request and the
job is killed by the 30-minute timeout. The vLLM servers start fine; only
the disaggregated request path hangs.

Pin the PD prefiller and decoder to MRV1 via VLLM_USE_V2_MODEL_RUNNER=0
until MRV2 supports KV connectors / reliably falls back to MRV1.

Signed-off-by: Samuel Shen <slshen@tensormesh.ai>

Signed-off-by: Feng Tian <feng.tian@intel.com>

* benchmarks: merge cachegen_xpu_e2e into test_cachegen and whitelist GPU backends

The xpu_e2e file was a superset of test_cachegen.py: same KV-blob /
serde construction logic, but it benchmarked both encode and decode
(plus a compression-ratio print), while test_cachegen.py only had a
decode bench (encode was commented out). It also wasn't actually
XPU-specific -- it used torch_device_type, so CUDA could run it too.

Merge encode + compression-ratio print into test_cachegen.py, unify the
chunk_size parameter set to [64, 128, 256, 768], and delete the
duplicate xpu_e2e file. Switch the skip gate from is_available() to an
explicit cuda/xpu whitelist so HPU / CPU-only CI / future backends
without a CacheGen kernel are skipped automatically.

Verified on Arc Pro B60 (XPU): all 8 benchmarks (encode/decode x
4 chunk sizes) pass with real timings.

Signed-off-by: Feng Tian <feng.tian@intel.com>

* sycl: add head_size kwarg and skip unimplemented ops in fallback tests

- csrc/sycl: add head_size=0 parameter to multi_layer_kv_transfer so the
  SYCL binding matches the CUDA c_ops signature; callers that pass
  head_size=... no longer raise TypeError on XPU. The argument is
  currently unused inside the SYCL kernel.
- tests/v1/test_python_ops_fallback.py: extend pin_memory branches to
  cover XPU (XPU supports tensor.pin_memory()), and add a per-backend
  skip in test_1_scenario so scenarios whose required op is missing from
  lmcache.xpu_ops are skipped instead of crashing the device with
  AttributeError/TypeError cascades.

Signed-off-by: Feng Tian <feng.tian@intel.com>

* tests: fix XPU fallback parity by selecting tensor mode per backend

The scenario helpers in test_python_ops_fallback.py decided between
pointer mode and tensor/tensor-list mode based on the device string,
which conflated the native XPU SYCL backend (which only accepts
uintptr_t pointers) with the Python fallback (which cannot dereference
XPU device pointers from ctypes and must receive torch.Tensor inputs).
Adding XPU to the tensor-mode branch made the native backend crash;
keeping XPU out made the fallback segfault.

Switch the decision to inspect the backend module: only the Python
fallback uses tensor/tensor-list mode on non-CPU/CUDA devices. This
keeps both xpu_sycl_ops and xpu_py_ops happy and lets test_2_compare
validate them against each other.

Also drop the obsolete block_size kwarg from _create_metadata in
test_xpu_connector.py to match the current KVLayerGroupsManager
signature.

Signed-off-by: Feng Tian <feng.tian@intel.com>

* tests: drop SCENARIO_REQUIRED_ATTR table, skip on AttributeError

The hand-maintained mapping of scenario name -> required ops attribute
was just a pre-check for what the scenario would do anyway on its
first ops.foo lookup. Replace it with a try/except AttributeError
around the scenario call: when a backend (e.g. lmcache.xpu_ops) does
not implement an op, the scenario raises AttributeError on first
access and we turn that into pytest.skip.

Same skip set as before (verified: 70 passed, 7 skipped on XPU).

Signed-off-by: Feng Tian <feng.tian@intel.com>

* test scenario adjustment to cover all xpu ops

- Align the XPU backend parameterization with the merged
  lmcache.c_ops module exposed by _get_backend(), and let scenarios
  surface real failures instead of being silently skipped on
  AttributeError.

Signed-off-by: Feng Tian <feng.tian@intel.com>

* fallback: make enum comparisons work across native/fallback boundary

The Python fallback ops compared `direction` and `gpu_kv_format` via
`==` / `in` against the fallback module's own Enum members. When the
merged `lmcache.c_ops` module is composed of a native backend
(`xpu_ops` / `c_ops`) plus the fallback, callers obtain the enum
members from the native pybind11 enums. Cross-type equality between
a pybind11 enum and a Python Enum / IntEnum is always False even
when the integer values match, so the fallback silently took the
wrong branch -- e.g. `single_layer_kv_transfer_sgl` on XPU executed
the D2H path for an H2D request, and `multi_layer_kv_transfer_unilateral`
mis-classified MLA formats and crashed on out-of-range indexing.

Compare by integer value at every callsite and promote
`TransferDirection` to `IntEnum` (matching `GPUKVFormat`) so that
`int(direction)` is well-defined. Also clean up the unilateral
scenario to decide tensor-list vs pointer mode based on whether the
actual resolved op is the fallback function (`fn is _py_ops.fn`)
rather than the merged module's name.

Signed-off-by: Feng Tian <feng.tian@intel.com>

* Revise test_python_ops_fallback changes to make minimal change

Signed-off-by: Feng Tian <feng.tian@intel.com>

* Sync missing GPUKVFormat enum field to align with master

Signed-off-by: Feng Tian <feng.tian@intel.com>

commits.author: deng451e
commits.message:
[CB] Token-level matching + per-token slot scatter for non-block-aligned KV reuse (#3582)

* [blend-v3] Token-level matching + per-token slot scatter for CB reuse

Match fingerprints at token stride (probe_stride=1) and scatter reused
KV with the per-token slot kernel (multi_layer_kv_transfer) instead of
matching/scattering at vLLM block granularity. This lets CacheBlend
reuse non-block-aligned matches, the common case for real workloads
where the shared body starts at an arbitrary token offset (a partial
vLLM block) rather than a chunk/block boundary.

- register_rope: probe_stride = 1 (find matches at any token offset)
- cb_unified_lookup: accept non-prefix matches at any cur_st (drop the
  chunk-alignment filter)
- cb_retrieve_pre_computed: per-token slot scatter of the full matched
  range. Partial vLLM blocks are written per slot, so matched and
  recomputed tokens sharing a block don't conflict. Removes the
  block-aligned drop checks and the now-dead whole-block scatter path.

Validated on prefix-suffix-tuner (non-block-aligned by construction):
~99% suffix hit, 3.91x TTFT vs full recompute, output matches the
full-recompute baseline. The slot kernel is bandwidth-bound and matches
the whole-block kernel's throughput (~700 GB/s), so no scatter overhead.

Signed-off-by: deng451e <838677410@qq.com>

* [blend-v3] Vectorize V3 matcher probe; drop obsolete probe stride

Token-level matching (probe_stride=1) had turned match_sub_sequence into
an O(tokens) pure-Python probe loop — ~5.7 ms at 32K context, ~7x the old
block-stride cost. Replace it with a vectorized direct-address probe
(numpy gather over all positions) plus a verify loop over only the
surviving hits; the table is sparse (TABLE_SIZE = 2^20 >> registered
chunks) so the hit set is tiny. This restores the base class's
vectorization that the V3 override had dropped, keeping full-hash
collision rejection.

Probe stride is now obsolete (we always scan every position), so the
_probe_stride field, ctor arg, and register_rope assignment are removed.

docs(mp): note non-CUDA auto-disable of --l1-use-lazy

PR #3259 (feat(mp): Non-GPU Context by pickle) added a post-init
guard in lmcache/v1/distributed/config.py:L1MemoryManagerConfig
that auto-disables use_lazy on backends where
lmcache.torch_dev has no cudart attribute, logging a warning
("LazyMemoryAllocator requires cudart which is not available
on the current backend. Disabling l1-use-lazy.").

The user-facing reference in docs/source/mp/configuration.rst
still listed the --l1-use-lazy default as plain "True" without
mentioning that non-CUDA backends silently downgrade to eager
allocation. Extend the description so the documented behavior
matches the actual code path.

  comment to reference `L2StoreResult` (success flag +

  reporting expectation that motivated the interface change.

  `total_bytes` to `bytes_transferred`, matching the new
  `L2ThroughputSubscriber._on_store_completed` path that reads the
  real-bytes field from `L2_STORE_COMPLETED` (and drops
  zero-byte samples). The load-throughput row picks up a short
  clarifying note that the load path still uses submitted
  `total_bytes`.

docs drift-check routine for 2026-05-26.

commits.author: Ilya Yanok
commits.message:
nixl_storage: naive support for files + dynamic (#2936)

* nixl_storage: naive support for files + dynamic

static is not actually very usable with files: we bump into the OS
limits on the open files very quickly, limiting the size of the cache.
With tons of VRAM, having G3 of comparable size is not helping much, we
really need it to be much bigger.

This commit adds support for files in dynamic mode of nixl storage.
There are some naive things done:
1. No support for sharing the cache storage. We assume the worker has
   exclusive access to the files and it is always safe to overwrite
   existing files. Note that with TP!=1 we will have several workers
   with the same target directory, that's why it's important to have a
   worker id as part of the key, so they don't affect each other.
2. No eviction support. As before, dynamic mode has no evicting support.
   This kinda made sense when it was only working with OBJ storages, but
   now that is something to be aware of. It's not hard to add eviction
   though.
3. Flat directory with all the cache files. There are going to be a lot
   of them, especially provided we don't have eviction. Most filesystems
   don't optimize for the case of a directory with millions of files,
   and we constantly open/close files there, that might be a source
   of additional latency.
   - There is an existing PR to support sharding the directory, we can
     merge it as a band aid.
   - As a more advanced solution, we can do multi-layer subdirectory
     structure, like gds backend does.
4. We switched directly from having _all_ files open at once, to
   open/close _every_ single file on access. We might explore having an
   LRU cache of open files instead.

commits.author: kumaneko
commits.message:
[Perf] Avoid redundant PCIe transfer on leader rank during retrieve (#3413)

* [Perf] Avoid redundant PCIe transfer on leader rank during retrieve

When `save_only_first_rank` is enabled (MLA path), the leader rank's
memory_objs returned from `_process_tokens_internal()` reference
CPU-resident L1 buffers. The broadcast step uploads each chunk to GPU
once via `raw_tensor.to(GPU)` so NCCL has a GPU send buffer, but the
result is discarded. The subsequent `batched_to_gpu` then reads the
same L1 bytes again over PCIe (via cudaHostGetDevicePointer zero-copy
mapping inside the multi_layer_kv_transfer kernel), making the leader
rank ~9 ms slower than passive ranks on every retrieve at our setup
(8192 tokens, tp=8, DeepSeek-R1-style MLA, H20 + PCIe Gen4).

This patch keeps the GPU-resident copy from the broadcast alive on the
leader and substitutes it as the source for `batched_to_gpu`, so the
kernel reads from HBM (~3 TB/s) instead of pinned host memory through
PCIe (~32 GB/s). The original CPU memory_objs remain in
`reordered_chunks` so the existing cleanup / ref-counting path is
unchanged. Passive ranks are unaffected (already pass GPU tensors).

Measured on tp=8 H20, DeepSeek-R1, 8192-token warm retrieve,
in-process connector:

| rank          | togpu_gpu before | after  |
|---------------|------------------|--------|
| 0  (leader)   | 9.42 ms          | 0.70 ms|
| 1-7 (passive) | 0.48 ms          | 0.48 ms|

End-to-end TTFT at c=1 improves by ~10 ms (~133 ms -> ~123 ms). All 8
ranks now finish `retrieve()` within ~0.2 ms of each other rather than
the leader trailing the others by ~9 ms.

Signed-off-by: kumaneko <crclq2018@gmail.com>

* [Perf] Move leader GPU substitute cleanup into try/finally

Address review feedback from gemini-code-assist on PR #3413: wrap the
batched_to_gpu call in try/finally so that the leader's
_leader_gpu_substitute_objs list is always cleared, even if
batched_to_gpu raises (e.g. CUDA OOM). Without this, a raise would
leave temporary GPU tensors held by the long-lived LMCacheEngine
until the next successful retrieve resets the list.

Signed-off-by: kumaneko <crclq2018@gmail.com>

* Fix mypy [has-type] error on _leader_gpu_substitute_objs

The previous patch declared self._leader_gpu_substitute_objs lazily
inside _broadcast_or_receive_memory_objs and accessed it via getattr/
hasattr in retrieve(). mypy could not infer the attribute's type
through that flow, failing the Code Quality CI check at line 871:

  Cannot determine type of "_leader_gpu_substitute_objs"  [has-type]

Per the LMCache coding standards ("Avoid Optional — initialize objects
even if empty"), declare the attribute in __init__ with its full type
annotation. The broadcast function now just resets it to a fresh list
at the start of the leader-side critical section, and the call site
reads it via plain attribute access (no getattr/hasattr guards).

Behavior is unchanged: non-leader ranks never populate the list, so
the substitution branch in retrieve() is gated by length-equality
against the chunk count, which is 0 on non-leaders and only matches
on the leader after the broadcast loop has populated it.

Verified with `pre-commit run --files lmcache/v1/cache_engine.py`:
all hooks (ruff, ruff-format, codespell, mypy, etc.) now pass.

Signed-off-by: kumaneko <crclq2018@gmail.com>

* Address review: skip list() on leader path; warn on substitute mismatch

Two cleanups from review feedback on PR #3413:

1. @maobaolong: hoist `memory_objs_for_togpu = list(memory_objs)` into
   the `else` branch so the leader-substitute path doesn't pay for an
   unnecessary `list()` allocation that gets immediately overwritten.

2. @chunxiaozheng: when on the leader rank with save_only_first_rank
   but the substitute list count does not match the memory_objs count,
   that indicates a bug or stale state (the broadcast should always
   have populated the substitute list to match). Log a warning and
   fall back to the CPU L1 source so retrieval is still correct but
   the anomaly is visible.

Also widen the static type of `_leader_gpu_substitute_objs` from
`List[TensorMemoryObj]` to `List[MemoryObj]` so mypy can see that
passing it to `batched_to_gpu(memory_objs: list[MemoryObj], ...)` is
type-safe (Python's `list` is invariant). The runtime objects are
still `TensorMemoryObj` instances built in `_broadcast_or_receive_
memory_objs`; only the static type widens.

Verified with `pre-commit run --files lmcache/v1/cache_engine.py`:
all hooks (ruff, ruff-format, mypy, codespell, isort, etc.) pass.

Signed-off-by: kumaneko <crclq2018@gmail.com>

commits.author: Guy Ealey Morag
commits.message:
nixl_storage: Fix tests and remove from ignore list (#3200)

* nixl_storage: naive support for files + dynamic

static is not actually very usable with files: we bump into the OS
limits on the open files very quickly, limiting the size of the cache.
With tons of VRAM, having G3 of comparable size is not helping much, we
really need it to be much bigger.

This commit adds support for files in dynamic mode of nixl storage.
There are some naive things done:
1. No support for sharing the cache storage. We assume the worker has
   exclusive access to the files and it is always safe to overwrite
   existing files. Note that with TP!=1 we will have several workers
   with the same target directory, that's why it's important to have a
   worker id as part of the key, so they don't affect each other.
2. No eviction support. As before, dynamic mode has no evicting support.
   This kinda made sense when it was only working with OBJ storages, but
   now that is something to be aware of. It's not hard to add eviction
   though.
3. Flat directory with all the cache files. There are going to be a lot
   of them, especially provided we don't have eviction. Most filesystems
   don't optimize for the case of a directory with millions of files,
   and we constantly open/close files there, that might be a source
   of additional latency.
   - There is an existing PR to support sharding the directory, we can
     merge it as a band aid.
   - As a more advanced solution, we can do multi-layer subdirectory
     structure, like gds backend does.
4. We switched directly from having _all_ files open at once, to
   open/close _every_ single file on access. We might explore having an
   LRU cache of open files instead.

Signed-off-by: Ilya Yanok <iyanok@nvidia.com>

commits.author: longguo
commits.message:
fix(docker): install CLI requirements in standalone image so `lmcache server` boots (fixes #3353) (#3355)

fix(docker): install CLI requirements in standalone image so `lmcache server` boots

`lmcache/standalone` (v0.4.5, latest, nightly) crashes on
`lmcache server` with:

    ModuleNotFoundError: No module named 'openai'

Root cause: `setup.py` only puts `requirements/common.txt` and
`requirements/cuda{12,13}_core.txt` into `install_requires`.
`requirements/cli.txt` -- which contains `openai` (used by the
`bench` engine client) -- is not installed.

`lmcache server` is registered via `lmcache.cli.main:main`, which
does `from lmcache.cli.commands import ALL_COMMANDS`. That triggers
`lmcache.cli.commands._discover_commands()`, which uses
`discover_subclasses(...)` to **eagerly importlib.import_module**
every submodule under `cli/commands/`. That includes
`bench/__init__.py`, whose top-level imports chain into
`engine_bench/request_sender.py:11 -> from openai import AsyncOpenAI`,
and the standalone image has no `openai`.

`cli/commands/__init__.py:18-30` deliberately re-raises import
errors ("a broken CLI command module should fail loudly rather than
silently disappear from the CLI"), so the discovery cannot be
softened on the slim install path -- the install path itself has
to be fixed.

The other Docker images (`Dockerfile`, `Dockerfile.rocm`,
`Dockerfile.lightweight`, `Dockerfile.rocm-lightweight`) all
install `vllm[runai,tensorizer,...]` or are `FROM vllm/vllm-openai*`,
and `openai` arrives transitively through vLLM there. Only the
standalone image is vLLM-free and hits this crash.

Minimal fix: install `requirements/cli.txt` alongside the existing
`ray nvidia-ml-py` step in the base stage, using a
`--mount=type=bind` so the file does not persist into the image
(matching the `build.txt` style used later by the lmcache-build
stage).

Out of scope (intentional, can be a follow-up): the deeper question
of whether `cli.txt` should be in `install_requires`, an
`extras_require["cli"]` block in `setup.py`, or whether the
fail-loudly contract in `cli/commands/__init__.py` should be
loosened, is an architectural call I'd defer to maintainers.
Either of those would also fix #3353 (and would fix the same crash
for users who `pip install lmcache` outside of Docker), but
keeping this PR scoped to the reported failure mode -- the standalone
Docker image -- keeps the review surface tight and unblocks the
broken images today.

Fixes #3353

Signed-off-by: abinggo <107740309+abinggo@users.noreply.github.com>

commits.author: GentleCold
commits.message:
[BugFix] fix sha256_cbor + async_loading type mismatch in _hash_tokens (#2999)

* [BugFix] fix sha256_cbor + async_loading type mismatch in _hash_tokens

When LMCACHE_PRE_CACHING_HASH_ALGORITHM=sha256_cbor is used together with
LMCACHE_ENABLE_ASYNC_LOADING=True, the async lookup path fails because
sha256_cbor (vLLM PR#23673) returns a 32-byte bytes digest. This digest is
placed into LookupRequestMsg.hashes which msgpack cannot encode as int,
raising OverflowError.

Fix: after calling hash_func in _hash_tokens, if the result is bytes,
fold the first 8 bytes into a uint64 int. This keeps all downstream code
(CacheEngineKey, msgpack serialisation) working with plain ints regardless
of the configured hash algorithm. Both the store path and the async lookup
path go through _hash_tokens, so the keys produced are always consistent.

Fixes #2997

Signed-off-by: GentleCold <gentlecold@qq.com>

* [BugFix] address review: fix NONE_HASH type and use public test interface

- token_database.py: convert NONE_HASH from bytes to int immediately after
  it is set from vLLM. This ensures the entire prefix-hash chain uses a
  consistent int type as canon_prefix input to hash_func, not bytes on the
  first chunk and int on subsequent ones.

- test_token_database.py: replace direct call to private _hash_tokens with
  the public process_tokens interface (make_key=False). Drop cbor2 import
  in favour of a simpler hashlib stub.

Signed-off-by: GentleCold <gentlecold@qq.com>

* [BugFix] normalize byte hash outputs in token database

- token_database.py: add a module-level helper to normalize hash outputs from vLLM hash functions into LMCache's int chunk-hash representation.

- Reuse the helper for both vLLM NONE_HASH initialization and token chunk hashing so sha256_cbor byte digests are folded consistently before entering the prefix hash chain.

* address review: merge cpu/gpu kv-cache allocator and fix chunk_size default

Per @hlin99 review on #3528: collapse _allocate_cpu_kv_cache and _allocate_gpu_kv_cache into a single _allocate_kv_cache(use_gpu=...). Most of the body was identical; the only divergence is whether to attach a CUDA device. Update command.py / tests to call the merged helper.

Per @yoo-kumaneko on #3528:

- args.transfer_mode is always set by argparse, so drop the getattr() defensive fallback. A future rename will now fail loudly instead of silently falling back to 'auto'.

- _send_register_kv_cache used to return 'bool | RegisterNonGpuContextResponse' and the caller relied on the response being truthy to mean success. RegisterNonGpuContextResponse is a dataclass with no __bool__, so even an empty failure response would print OK. commits.author: deng451e
commits.message:
[CB] Token-level matching + per-token slot scatter for non-block-aligned KV reuse (#3582)

* [blend-v3] Token-level matching + per-token slot scatter for CB reuse

Match fingerprints at token stride (probe_stride=1) and scatter reused
KV with the per-token slot kernel (multi_layer_kv_transfer) instead of
matching/scattering at vLLM block granularity. This lets CacheBlend
reuse non-block-aligned matches, the common case for real workloads
where the shared body starts at an arbitrary token offset (a partial
vLLM block) rather than a chunk/block boundary.

- register_rope: probe_stride = 1 (find matches at any token offset)
- cb_unified_lookup: accept non-prefix matches at any cur_st (drop the
  chunk-alignment filter)
- cb_retrieve_pre_computed: per-token slot scatter of the full matched
  range. Partial vLLM blocks are written per slot, so matched and
  recomputed tokens sharing a block don't conflict. Removes the
  block-aligned drop checks and the now-dead whole-block scatter path.

Validated on prefix-suffix-tuner (non-block-aligned by construction):
~99% suffix hit, 3.91x TTFT vs full recompute, output matches the
full-recompute baseline. The slot kernel is bandwidth-bound and matches
the whole-block kernel's throughput (~700 GB/s), so no scatter overhead.

Signed-off-by: deng451e <838677410@qq.com>

* [blend-v3] Vectorize V3 matcher probe; drop obsolete probe stride

Token-level matching (probe_stride=1) had turned match_sub_sequence into
an O(tokens) pure-Python probe loop — ~5.7 ms at 32K context, ~7x the old
block-stride cost. Replace it with a vectorized direct-address probe
(numpy gather over all positions) plus a verify loop over only the
surviving hits; the table is sparse (TABLE_SIZE = 2^20 >> registered
chunks) so the hit set is tiny. This restores the base class's
vectorization that the V3 override had dropped, keeping full-hash
collision rejection.

Probe stride is now obsolete (we always scan every position), so the
_probe_stride field, ctor arg, and register_rope assignment are removed.

Matcher microbench (CPU, per lookup): 32K ctx 5.66 -> 0.83 ms (~7x),
20K 3.43 -> 0.52, 8K 1.39 -> 0.23 — back to the pre-token-scatter
block-stride baseline with full token-level matching. All 20
test_optimized_lookup_v3 tests pass.

Signed-off-by: deng451e <838677410@qq.com>

* update

* update stale docstring

commits.author: Samuel Shen
commits.message:
[Chore][CI] Force vLLM Model Runner V1 in the PD comprehensive test (#3502)

[CI/CD] Force vLLM Model Runner V1 in the PD comprehensive test

The PD comprehensive test (k3-comprehensive-test) started timing out on
2026-06-02 when the floating vLLM nightly bumped from 0.22.1rc1.dev91 to
.dev95. That delta includes vllm-project/vllm#43458, which enables Model
Runner V2 (MRV2) by default for Llama/Mistral dense models -- including
meta-llama/Llama-3.2-1B-Instruct, the model used by pd.yaml.

MRV2 is incompatible with KV cache connectors, so the prefiller/decoder
(both driven through LMCacheConnectorV1) hang on the first request and the
job is killed by the 30-minute timeout. The vLLM servers start fine; only
the disaggregated request path hangs.

Pin the PD prefiller and decoder to MRV1 via VLLM_USE_V2_MODEL_RUNNER=0
until MRV2 supports KV connectors / reliably falls back to MRV1.

Signed-off-by: Samuel Shen <slshen@tensormesh.ai>

commits.author: Tian, Feng
commits.message:
[XPU] Add SYCL CacheGen + RoPE kernels and in-process blender XPU tests (#3360)

* [XPU] Add SYCL CacheGen + RoPE kernels and in-process blender XPU tests

Implements native SYCL/DPC++ kernels for the four CacheGen + RoPE entry
points on Intel XPU, exposed through lmcache.xpu_ops and transparently
picked up by _get_backend():

- cal_cdf_sycl.cpp        : calculate_cdf

- ac_dec_sycl.cpp         : decode_fast_new / decode_fast_prefsum

Signed-off-by: Feng Tian <feng.tian@intel.com>

* Add input validation TORCH_CHECKs to encoder/decoder

- dtype checks for cdf/input/output/lengths tensors
- dim checks before accessing sizes()[N]
- lp >= 2 (max_symbol = lp - 2 must be non-negative)
- bytestreams last dim == OUTPUT_BUFFER_LENGTH_PER_THREAD (decoder)
- lengths.max() <= OUTPUT_BUFFER_LENGTH_PER_THREAD (decoder, prevents
  per-channel kernel OOB read on bs_shared)

Signed-off-by: Feng Tian <feng.tian@intel.com>

* test_serde: whitelist CUDA/XPU by availability

Signed-off-by: Feng Tian <feng.tian@intel.com>

* test_python_ops_fallback: add xpu_sycl_ops backend for SYCL parity check

Signed-off-by: Feng Tian <feng.tian@intel.com>

* benchmarks: merge cachegen_xpu_e2e into test_cachegen and whitelist GPU backends

The xpu_e2e file was a superset of test_cachegen.py: same KV-blob /
serde construction logic, but it benchmarked both encode and decode
(plus a compression-ratio print), while test_cachegen.py only had a
decode bench (encode was commented out). It also wasn't actually
XPU-specific -- it used torch_device_type, so CUDA could run it too.

Merge encode + compression-ratio print into test_cachegen.py, unify the
chunk_size parameter set to [64, 128, 256, 768], and delete the
duplicate xpu_e2e file. Switch the skip gate from is_available() to an
explicit cuda/xpu whitelist so HPU / CPU-only CI / future backends
without a CacheGen kernel are skipped automatically.

Verified on Arc Pro B60 (XPU): all 8 benchmarks (encode/decode x
4 chunk sizes) pass with real timings.

Signed-off-by: Feng Tian <feng.tian@intel.com>

* sycl: add head_size kwarg and skip unimplemented ops in fallback tests

- csrc/sycl: add head_size=0 parameter to multi_layer_kv_transfer so the
  SYCL binding matches the CUDA c_ops signature; callers that pass
  head_size=... no longer raise TypeError on XPU. The argument is
  currently unused inside the SYCL kernel.
- tests/v1/test_python_ops_fallback.py: extend pin_memory branches to
  cover XPU (XPU supports tensor.pin_memory()), and add a per-backend
  skip in test_1_scenario so scenarios whose required op is missing from
  lmcache.xpu_ops are skipped instead of crashing the device with
  AttributeError/TypeError cascades.

Signed-off-by: Feng Tian <feng.tian@intel.com>

* tests: fix XPU fallback parity by selecting tensor mode per backend

The scenario helpers in test_python_ops_fallback.py decided between
pointer mode and tensor/tensor-list mode based on the device string,
which conflated the native XPU SYCL backend (which only accepts
uintptr_t pointers) with the Python fallback (which cannot dereference
XPU device pointers from ctypes and must receive torch.Tensor inputs).
Adding XPU to the tensor-mode branch made the native backend crash;
keeping XPU out made the fallback segfault.

Switch the decision to inspect the backend module: only the Python
fallback uses tensor/tensor-list mode on non-CPU/CUDA devices. This
keeps both xpu_sycl_ops and xpu_py_ops happy and lets test_2_compare
validate them against each other.

Also drop the obsolete block_size kwarg from _create_metadata in
test_xpu_connector.py to match the current KVLayerGroupsManager
signature.

Signed-off-by: Feng Tian <feng.tian@intel.com>

* tests: drop SCENARIO_REQUIRED_ATTR table, skip on AttributeError

The hand-maintained mapping of scenario name -> required ops attribute
was just a pre-check for what the scenario would do anyway on its
first ops.foo lookup. Replace it with a try/except AttributeError
around the scenario call: when a backend (e.g. lmcache.xpu_ops) does
not implement an op, the scenario raises AttributeError on first
access and we turn that into pytest.skip.

Same skip set as before (verified: 70 passed, 7 skipped on XPU).

Signed-off-by: Feng Tian <feng.tian@intel.com>

* test scenario adjustment to cover all xpu ops

- Align the XPU backend parameterization with the merged
  lmcache.c_ops module exposed by _get_backend(), and let scenarios
  surface real failures instead of being silently skipped on
  AttributeError.

Signed-off-by: Feng Tian <feng.tian@intel.com>

* fallback: make enum comparisons work across native/fallback boundary

The Python fallback ops compared `direction` and `gpu_kv_format` via
`==` / `in` against the fallback module's own Enum members. When the
merged `lmcache.c_ops` module is composed of a native backend
(`xpu_ops` / `c_ops`) plus the fallback, callers obtain the enum
members from the native pybind11 enums. Cross-type equality between
a pybind11 enum and a Python Enum / IntEnum is always False even
when the integer values match, so the fallback silently took the
wrong branch -- e.g. `single_layer_kv_transfer_sgl` on XPU executed
the D2H path for an H2D request, and `multi_layer_kv_transfer_unilateral`
mis-classified MLA formats and crashed on out-of-range indexing.

Compare by integer value at every callsite and promote
`TransferDirection` to `IntEnum` (matching `GPUKVFormat`) so that
`int(direction)` is well-defined. Also clean up the unilateral
scenario to decide tensor-list vs pointer mode based on whether the
actual resolved op is the fallback function (`fn is _py_ops.fn`)
rather than the merged module's name.

Signed-off-by: Feng Tian <feng.tian@intel.com>

* Revise test_python_ops_fallback changes to make minimal change

Signed-off-by: Feng Tian <feng.tian@intel.com>

* Sync missing GPUKVFormat enum field to align with master

commits.message:
feat(bench): server_bench supports --mode cpu and --transfer-mode (#3528)

* feat(bench): server_bench supports --mode cpu and --transfer-mode

- --mode cpu: allocate POSIX-SHM-backed KV cache tensors via
  CpuShmTensorWrapper; the server maps the same physical pages
  for zero-copy STORE/RETRIEVE.
- --transfer-mode {auto,handle,data}: explicit override of the
  routing. auto keeps the historical gpu->handle / cpu->data
  dispatch. data uses REGISTER_KV_CACHE_NON_GPU_CONTEXT +
  PREPARE/COMMIT two-phase path with client-side self-check.
- handle mode on CPU is left as TODO for a follow-up PR.
- docs: add Bench Server section to configuration.rst.

Signed-off-by: baoloongmao <baoloongmao@tencent.com>

* address review: merge cpu/gpu kv-cache allocator and fix chunk_size default

Per @hlin99 review on #3528: collapse _allocate_cpu_kv_cache and _allocate_gpu_kv_cache into a single _allocate_kv_cache(use_gpu=...). Most of the body was identical; the only divergence is whether to attach a CUDA device. Update command.py / tests to call the merged helper.

Also bump _send_store(chunk_size=...) default from 0 to 256 to match the typical server-side chunk size; the existing chunk_size > 0 guard is preserved by callers passing the runtime value explicitly.

Signed-off-by: baoloongmao <baoloongmao@tencent.com>

* address review: drop defensive getattr, return register tuple

Per @yoo-kumaneko on #3528:

- args.transfer_mode is always set by argparse, so drop the getattr() defensive fallback. A future rename will now fail loudly instead of silently falling back to 'auto'.

- _send_register_kv_cache used to return 'bool | RegisterNonGpuContextResponse' and the caller relied on the response being truthy to mean success. RegisterNonGpuContextResponse is a dataclass with no __bool__, so even an empty failure response would print OK. Switch to (success, Optional[response]) so the success flag is explicit, and treat an empty shm_name as failure.

Signed-off-by: baoloongmao <baoloongmao@tencent.com>

ensure shm_create_readwrite / shm_map_readwrite never leak the fd
or the mmap when ftruncate or mmap fails. also rename _nbytes to

test can simulate id recycling without poking module-private

commits.message:
[CB] Blend v3 (#3364)

* [blend-v3] CacheBlend V3 integration on the sparse-prefetch path

Rebuilt on #3492 (sparse prefetch) after #3491 merged into dev. Adds the
paged-aware CB pipeline: blend_v3 server module + protocol
(CB_REGISTER_ROPE_V3, CB_RETRIEVE_PRE_COMPUTED_V3, CB_UNIFIED_LOOKUP),
CB custom types, server wiring, and the load/store + optimized-lookup
tests. Storage/codec layers are inherited unchanged from #3491/#3492.

Adapts to the upstream gpu_context API rename: stage_block_ids ->
copy_view_block_ids_to_gpu (per-KV-group; single-group call here).

 Signed-off-by: deng451e <838677410@qq.com>

* [blend-v3] Fix STORE handler signature to list[list[int]]

The upstream STORE protocol passes gpu_block_ids as per-group block lists
(list[list[int]]) under the HMA refactor; the flat list[int] annotation
failed handler-signature validation at blend-server boot (ValueError:
Handler signature does not match for RequestType.STORE), crashing the
rebased stack. Match the protocol so the server boots.

Signed-off-by: deng451e <838677410@qq.com>

commits.author: deng451e
commits.message:
[CB] Token-level matching + per-token slot scatter for non-block-aligned KV reuse (#3582)

* [blend-v3] Token-level matching + per-token slot scatter for CB reuse

Match fingerprints at token stride (probe_stride=1) and scatter reused
KV with the per-token slot kernel (multi_layer_kv_transfer) instead of
matching/scattering at vLLM block granularity. This lets CacheBlend
reuse non-block-aligned matches, the common case for real workloads
where the shared body starts at an arbitrary token offset (a partial
vLLM block) rather than a chunk/block boundary.

- register_rope: probe_stride = 1 (find matches at any token offset)
- cb_unified_lookup: accept non-prefix matches at any cur_st (drop the
  chunk-alignment filter)
- cb_retrieve_pre_computed: per-token slot scatter of the full matched
  range. Partial vLLM blocks are written per slot, so matched and
  recomputed tokens sharing a block don't conflict. Removes the
  block-aligned drop checks and the now-dead whole-block scatter path.

Validated on prefix-suffix-tuner (non-block-aligned by construction):
~99% suffix hit, 3.91x TTFT vs full recompute, output matches the
full-recompute baseline. The slot kernel is bandwidth-bound and matches
the whole-block kernel's throughput (~700 GB/s), so no scatter overhead.

Signed-off-by: deng451e <838677410@qq.com>

* [blend-v3] Vectorize V3 matcher probe; drop obsolete probe stride

Token-level matching (probe_stride=1) had turned match_sub_sequence into
an O(tokens) pure-Python probe loop — ~5.7 ms at 32K context, ~7x the old
block-stride cost. Replace it with a vectorized direct-address probe
(numpy gather over all positions) plus a verify loop over only the
surviving hits; the table is sparse (TABLE_SIZE = 2^20 >> registered
chunks) so the hit set is tiny. This restores the base class's
vectorization that the V3 override had dropped, keeping full-hash
collision rejection.

Probe stride is now obsolete (we always scan every position), so the
_probe_stride field, ctor arg, and register_rope assignment are removed.

Matcher microbench (CPU, per lookup): 32K ctx 5.66 -> 0.83 ms (~7x),
20K 3.43 -> 0.52, 8K 1.39 -> 0.23 — back to the pre-token-scatter
block-stride baseline with full token-level matching. All 20
test_optimized_lookup_v3 tests pass.

Signed-off-by: deng451e <838677410@qq.com>

* update stale docstring

commits.author: Ilya Yanok
commits.message:
nixl_storage: naive support for files + dynamic (#2936)

* nixl_storage: naive support for files + dynamic

static is not actually very usable with files: we bump into the OS
limits on the open files very quickly, limiting the size of the cache.
With tons of VRAM, having G3 of comparable size is not helping much, we
really need it to be much bigger.

This commit adds support for files in dynamic mode of nixl storage.
There are some naive things done:
1. No support for sharing the cache storage. We assume the worker has
   exclusive access to the files and it is always safe to overwrite
   existing files. Note that with TP!=1 we will have several workers
   with the same target directory, that's why it's important to have a
   worker id as part of the key, so they don't affect each other.
2. No eviction support. As before, dynamic mode has no evicting support.
   This kinda made sense when it was only working with OBJ storages, but
   now that is something to be aware of. It's not hard to add eviction
   though.
3. Flat directory with all the cache files. There are going to be a lot
   of them, especially provided we don't have eviction. Most filesystems
   don't optimize for the case of a directory with millions of files,
   and we constantly open/close files there, that might be a source
   of additional latency.
   - There is an existing PR to support sharding the directory, we can
     merge it as a band aid.
   - As a more advanced solution, we can do multi-layer subdirectory
     structure, like gds backend does.
4. We switched directly from having _all_ files open at once, to
   open/close _every_ single file on access. We might explore having an
   LRU cache of open files instead.

commits.author: Samuel Shen
commits.message:
[Chore][CI] Force vLLM Model Runner V1 in the PD comprehensive test (#3502)

[CI/CD] Force vLLM Model Runner V1 in the PD comprehensive test

The PD comprehensive test (k3-comprehensive-test) started timing out on
2026-06-02 when the floating vLLM nightly bumped from 0.22.1rc1.dev91 to
.dev95. That delta includes vllm-project/vllm#43458, which enables Model
Runner V2 (MRV2) by default for Llama/Mistral dense models -- including
meta-llama/Llama-3.2-1B-Instruct, the model used by pd.yaml.

MRV2 is incompatible with KV cache connectors, so the prefiller/decoder
(both driven through LMCacheConnectorV1) hang on the first request and the
job is killed by the 30-minute timeout. The vLLM servers start fine; only
the disaggregated request path hangs.

Pin the PD prefiller and decoder to MRV1 via VLLM_USE_V2_MODEL_RUNNER=0
until MRV2 supports KV connectors / reliably falls back to MRV1.

Signed-off-by: Samuel Shen <slshen@tensormesh.ai>

Add sycl implementation of memory_kernels for Intel XPU (#2902)

* Add SYCL/XPU port of mem_kernels with Intel optimizations and build scripts

Performance optimization for flash_infer on Intel XPU:

1. Added page_buffer_base_offset() and key_value_base_offset() helpers
   that compute loop-invariant base offsets (incl. integer division for
   flash_infer block mapping) once before the inner loop.

2. Added submit_multi_layer_kernel_fused_kv() that processes both K and V

   and avoiding redundant slot lookups, pointer reads, and division.

3. Updated dispatch logic to use fused kernel for non-MLA formats
   (flash_infer, flash_attention, cross-layer) and original kernel for
   MLA formats.

commits.message:
[XPU] Add SYCL CacheGen + RoPE kernels and in-process blender XPU tests (#3360)

* [XPU] Add SYCL CacheGen + RoPE kernels and in-process blender XPU tests

Implements native SYCL/DPC++ kernels for the four CacheGen + RoPE entry
points on Intel XPU, exposed through lmcache.xpu_ops and transparently
picked up by _get_backend():

- cal_cdf_sycl.cpp        : calculate_cdf
- ac_enc_sycl.cpp         : encode_fast_new
- ac_dec_sycl.cpp         : decode_fast_new / decode_fast_prefsum
- pos_kernels_sycl.cpp    : rotary_embedding_k_fused

Signed-off-by: Feng Tian <feng.tian@intel.com>

* Add input validation TORCH_CHECKs to encoder/decoder

- dtype checks for cdf/input/output/lengths tensors
- dim checks before accessing sizes()[N]
- lp >= 2 (max_symbol = lp - 2 must be non-negative)
- bytestreams last dim == OUTPUT_BUFFER_LENGTH_PER_THREAD (decoder)
- lengths.max() <= OUTPUT_BUFFER_LENGTH_PER_THREAD (decoder, prevents
  per-channel kernel OOB read on bs_shared)

Signed-off-by: Feng Tian <feng.tian@intel.com>

* test_serde: whitelist CUDA/XPU by availability

Signed-off-by: Feng Tian <feng.tian@intel.com>

* test_python_ops_fallback: add xpu_sycl_ops backend for SYCL parity check

Signed-off-by: Feng Tian <feng.tian@intel.com>

* benchmarks: merge cachegen_xpu_e2e into test_cachegen and whitelist GPU backends

The xpu_e2e file was a superset of test_cachegen.py: same KV-blob /
serde construction logic, but it benchmarked both encode and decode
(plus a compression-ratio print), while test_cachegen.py only had a
decode bench (encode was commented out). It also wasn't actually
XPU-specific -- it used torch_device_type, so CUDA could run it too.

Merge encode + compression-ratio print into test_cachegen.py, unify the
chunk_size parameter set to [64, 128, 256, 768], and delete the
duplicate xpu_e2e file. Switch the skip gate from is_available() to an
explicit cuda/xpu whitelist so HPU / CPU-only CI / future backends
without a CacheGen kernel are skipped automatically.

Verified on Arc Pro B60 (XPU): all 8 benchmarks (encode/decode x
4 chunk sizes) pass with real timings.

Signed-off-by: Feng Tian <feng.tian@intel.com>

* sycl: add head_size kwarg and skip unimplemented ops in fallback tests

- csrc/sycl: add head_size=0 parameter to multi_layer_kv_transfer so the
  SYCL binding matches the CUDA c_ops signature; callers that pass
  head_size=... no longer raise TypeError on XPU. The argument is
  currently unused inside the SYCL kernel.
- tests/v1/test_python_ops_fallback.py: extend pin_memory branches to
  cover XPU (XPU supports tensor.pin_memory()), and add a per-backend
  skip in test_1_scenario so scenarios whose required op is missing from
  lmcache.xpu_ops are skipped instead of crashing the device with
  AttributeError/TypeError cascades.

Signed-off-by: Feng Tian <feng.tian@intel.com>

* tests: fix XPU fallback parity by selecting tensor mode per backend

The scenario helpers in test_python_ops_fallback.py decided between
pointer mode and tensor/tensor-list mode based on the device string,
which conflated the native XPU SYCL backend (which only accepts
uintptr_t pointers) with the Python fallback (which cannot dereference
XPU device pointers from ctypes and must receive torch.Tensor inputs).
Adding XPU to the tensor-mode branch made the native backend crash;
keeping XPU out made the fallback segfault.

Switch the decision to inspect the backend module: only the Python
fallback uses tensor/tensor-list mode on non-CPU/CUDA devices. This
keeps both xpu_sycl_ops and xpu_py_ops happy and lets test_2_compare
validate them against each other.

Also drop the obsolete block_size kwarg from _create_metadata in
test_xpu_connector.py to match the current KVLayerGroupsManager
signature.

Signed-off-by: Feng Tian <feng.tian@intel.com>

* tests: drop SCENARIO_REQUIRED_ATTR table, skip on AttributeError

The hand-maintained mapping of scenario name -> required ops attribute
was just a pre-check for what the scenario would do anyway on its
first ops.foo lookup. Replace it with a try/except AttributeError
around the scenario call: when a backend (e.g. lmcache.xpu_ops) does
not implement an op, the scenario raises AttributeError on first
access and we turn that into pytest.skip.

Same skip set as before (verified: 70 passed, 7 skipped on XPU).

Signed-off-by: Feng Tian <feng.tian@intel.com>

* test scenario adjustment to cover all xpu ops

- Align the XPU backend parameterization with the merged
  lmcache.c_ops module exposed by _get_backend(), and let scenarios
  surface real failures instead of being silently skipped on
  AttributeError.

Signed-off-by: Feng Tian <feng.tian@intel.com>

* fallback: make enum comparisons work across native/fallback boundary

The Python fallback ops compared `direction` and `gpu_kv_format` via
`==` / `in` against the fallback module's own Enum members. When the
merged `lmcache.c_ops` module is composed of a native backend
(`xpu_ops` / `c_ops`) plus the fallback, callers obtain the enum
members from the native pybind11 enums. Cross-type equality between
a pybind11 enum and a Python Enum / IntEnum is always False even
when the integer values match, so the fallback silently took the
wrong branch -- e.g. `single_layer_kv_transfer_sgl` on XPU executed
the D2H path for an H2D request, and `multi_layer_kv_transfer_unilateral`
mis-classified MLA formats and crashed on out-of-range indexing.

Compare by integer value at every callsite and promote
`TransferDirection` to `IntEnum` (matching `GPUKVFormat`) so that
`int(direction)` is well-defined. Also clean up the unilateral
scenario to decide tensor-list vs pointer mode based on whether the
actual resolved op is the fallback function (`fn is _py_ops.fn`)
rather than the merged module's name.

Signed-off-by: Feng Tian <feng.tian@intel.com>

* Revise test_python_ops_fallback changes to make minimal change

Signed-off-by: Feng Tian <feng.tian@intel.com>

* Sync missing GPUKVFormat enum field to align with master

Signed-off-by: Feng Tian <feng.tian@intel.com>

commits.author: kumaneko
commits.message:
[Perf] Avoid redundant PCIe transfer on leader rank during retrieve (#3413)

* [Perf] Avoid redundant PCIe transfer on leader rank during retrieve

When `save_only_first_rank` is enabled (MLA path), the leader rank's
memory_objs returned from `_process_tokens_internal()` reference
CPU-resident L1 buffers. The broadcast step uploads each chunk to GPU
once via `raw_tensor.to(GPU)` so NCCL has a GPU send buffer, but the
result is discarded. The subsequent `batched_to_gpu` then reads the
same L1 bytes again over PCIe (via cudaHostGetDevicePointer zero-copy
mapping inside the multi_layer_kv_transfer kernel), making the leader
rank ~9 ms slower than passive ranks on every retrieve at our setup
(8192 tokens, tp=8, DeepSeek-R1-style MLA, H20 + PCIe Gen4).

This patch keeps the GPU-resident copy from the broadcast alive on the
leader and substitutes it as the source for `batched_to_gpu`, so the
kernel reads from HBM (~3 TB/s) instead of pinned host memory through
PCIe (~32 GB/s). The original CPU memory_objs remain in
`reordered_chunks` so the existing cleanup / ref-counting path is
unchanged. Passive ranks are unaffected (already pass GPU tensors).

Measured on tp=8 H20, DeepSeek-R1, 8192-token warm retrieve,
in-process connector:

| rank          | togpu_gpu before | after  |
|---------------|------------------|--------|
| 0  (leader)   | 9.42 ms          | 0.70 ms|
| 1-7 (passive) | 0.48 ms          | 0.48 ms|

End-to-end TTFT at c=1 improves by ~10 ms (~133 ms -> ~123 ms). All 8
ranks now finish `retrieve()` within ~0.2 ms of each other rather than
the leader trailing the others by ~9 ms.

Signed-off-by: kumaneko <crclq2018@gmail.com>

* [Perf] Move leader GPU substitute cleanup into try/finally

Address review feedback from gemini-code-assist on PR #3413: wrap the
batched_to_gpu call in try/finally so that the leader's
_leader_gpu_substitute_objs list is always cleared, even if
batched_to_gpu raises (e.g. CUDA OOM). Without this, a raise would
leave temporary GPU tensors held by the long-lived LMCacheEngine
until the next successful retrieve resets the list.

Signed-off-by: kumaneko <crclq2018@gmail.com>

* Fix mypy [has-type] error on _leader_gpu_substitute_objs

The previous patch declared self._leader_gpu_substitute_objs lazily
inside _broadcast_or_receive_memory_objs and accessed it via getattr/
hasattr in retrieve(). mypy could not infer the attribute's type
through that flow, failing the Code Quality CI check at line 871:

  Cannot determine type of "_leader_gpu_substitute_objs"  [has-type]

Per the LMCache coding standards ("Avoid Optional — initialize objects
even if empty"), declare the attribute in __init__ with its full type
annotation. The broadcast function now just resets it to a fresh list
at the start of the leader-side critical section, and the call site
reads it via plain attribute access (no getattr/hasattr guards).

Behavior is unchanged: non-leader ranks never populate the list, so
the substitution branch in retrieve() is gated by length-equality
against the chunk count, which is 0 on non-leaders and only matches
on the leader after the broadcast loop has populated it.

Verified with `pre-commit run --files lmcache/v1/cache_engine.py`:
all hooks (ruff, ruff-format, codespell, mypy, etc.) now pass.

Signed-off-by: kumaneko <crclq2018@gmail.com>

* Address review: skip list() on leader path; warn on substitute mismatch

Two cleanups from review feedback on PR #3413:

1. @maobaolong: hoist `memory_objs_for_togpu = list(memory_objs)` into
   the `else` branch so the leader-substitute path doesn't pay for an
   unnecessary `list()` allocation that gets immediately overwritten.

2. @chunxiaozheng: when on the leader rank with save_only_first_rank
   but the substitute list count does not match the memory_objs count,
   that indicates a bug or stale state (the broadcast should always
   have populated the substitute list to match). Log a warning and
   fall back to the CPU L1 source so retrieval is still correct but
   the anomaly is visible.

Also widen the static type of `_leader_gpu_substitute_objs` from
`List[TensorMemoryObj]` to `List[MemoryObj]` so mypy can see that
passing it to `batched_to_gpu(memory_objs: list[MemoryObj], ...)` is
type-safe (Python's `list` is invariant). The runtime objects are
still `TensorMemoryObj` instances built in `_broadcast_or_receive_
memory_objs`; only the static type widens.

Verified with `pre-commit run --files lmcache/v1/cache_engine.py`:
all hooks (ruff, ruff-format, mypy, codespell, isort, etc.) pass.

Signed-off-by: kumaneko <crclq2018@gmail.com>

commits.author: Guy Ealey Morag
commits.message:
nixl_storage: Fix tests and remove from ignore list (#3200)

* nixl_storage: naive support for files + dynamic

static is not actually very usable with files: we bump into the OS
limits on the open files very quickly, limiting the size of the cache.
With tons of VRAM, having G3 of comparable size is not helping much, we
really need it to be much bigger.

This commit adds support for files in dynamic mode of nixl storage.
There are some naive things done:
1. No support for sharing the cache storage. We assume the worker has
   exclusive access to the files and it is always safe to overwrite
   existing files. Note that with TP!=1 we will have several workers
   with the same target directory, that's why it's important to have a
   worker id as part of the key, so they don't affect each other.
2. No eviction support. As before, dynamic mode has no evicting support.
   This kinda made sense when it was only working with OBJ storages, but
   now that is something to be aware of. It's not hard to add eviction
   though.
3. Flat directory with all the cache files. There are going to be a lot
   of them, especially provided we don't have eviction. Most filesystems
   don't optimize for the case of a directory with millions of files,
   and we constantly open/close files there, that might be a source
   of additional latency.
   - There is an existing PR to support sharding the directory, we can
     merge it as a band aid.
   - As a more advanced solution, we can do multi-layer subdirectory
     structure, like gds backend does.
4. We switched directly from having _all_ files open at once, to
   open/close _every_ single file on access. We might explore having an
   LRU cache of open files instead.

commits.author: longguo
commits.message:
fix(docker): install CLI requirements in standalone image so `lmcache server` boots (fixes #3353) (#3355)

fix(docker): install CLI requirements in standalone image so `lmcache server` boots

`lmcache/standalone` (v0.4.5, latest, nightly) crashes on
`lmcache server` with:

    ModuleNotFoundError: No module named 'openai'

Root cause: `setup.py` only puts `requirements/common.txt` and
`requirements/cuda{12,13}_core.txt` into `install_requires`.
`requirements/cli.txt` -- which contains `openai` (used by the
`bench` engine client) -- is not installed.

`lmcache server` is registered via `lmcache.cli.main:main`, which
does `from lmcache.cli.commands import ALL_COMMANDS`. That triggers
`lmcache.cli.commands._discover_commands()`, which uses
`discover_subclasses(...)` to **eagerly importlib.import_module**
every submodule under `cli/commands/`. That includes
`bench/__init__.py`, whose top-level imports chain into
`engine_bench/request_sender.py:11 -> from openai import AsyncOpenAI`,
and the standalone image has no `openai`.

`cli/commands/__init__.py:18-30` deliberately re-raises import
errors ("a broken CLI command module should fail loudly rather than
silently disappear from the CLI"), so the discovery cannot be
softened on the slim install path -- the install path itself has
to be fixed.

The other Docker images (`Dockerfile`, `Dockerfile.rocm`,
`Dockerfile.lightweight`, `Dockerfile.rocm-lightweight`) all
install `vllm[runai,tensorizer,...]` or are `FROM vllm/vllm-openai*`,
and `openai` arrives transitively through vLLM there. Only the
standalone image is vLLM-free and hits this crash.

Minimal fix: install `requirements/cli.txt` alongside the existing
`ray nvidia-ml-py` step in the base stage, using a
`--mount=type=bind` so the file does not persist into the image
(matching the `build.txt` style used later by the lmcache-build
stage).

Out of scope (intentional, can be a follow-up): the deeper question
of whether `cli.txt` should be in `install_requires`, an
`extras_require["cli"]` block in `setup.py`, or whether the
fail-loudly contract in `cli/commands/__init__.py` should be
loosened, is an architectural call I'd defer to maintainers.
Either of those would also fix #3353 (and would fix the same crash
for users who `pip install lmcache` outside of Docker), but
keeping this PR scoped to the reported failure mode -- the standalone
Docker image -- keeps the review surface tight and unblocks the
broken images today.

Fixes #3353

commits.author: GentleCold
commits.message:
[BugFix] fix sha256_cbor + async_loading type mismatch in _hash_tokens (#2999)

* [BugFix] fix sha256_cbor + async_loading type mismatch in _hash_tokens

When LMCACHE_PRE_CACHING_HASH_ALGORITHM=sha256_cbor is used together with
LMCACHE_ENABLE_ASYNC_LOADING=True, the async lookup path fails because
sha256_cbor (vLLM PR#23673) returns a 32-byte bytes digest. This digest is
placed into LookupRequestMsg.hashes which msgpack cannot encode as int,
raising OverflowError.

Fix: after calling hash_func in _hash_tokens, if the result is bytes,
fold the first 8 bytes into a uint64 int. This keeps all downstream code
(CacheEngineKey, msgpack serialisation) working with plain ints regardless
of the configured hash algorithm. Both the store path and the async lookup
path go through _hash_tokens, so the keys produced are always consistent.

Fixes #2997

Signed-off-by: GentleCold <gentlecold@qq.com>

* [BugFix] address review: fix NONE_HASH type and use public test interface

- token_database.py: convert NONE_HASH from bytes to int immediately after
  it is set from vLLM. This ensures the entire prefix-hash chain uses a
  consistent int type as canon_prefix input to hash_func, not bytes on the
  first chunk and int on subsequent ones.

- test_token_database.py: replace direct call to private _hash_tokens with
  the public process_tokens interface (make_key=False). Drop cbor2 import
  in favour of a simpler hashlib stub.

Signed-off-by: GentleCold <gentlecold@qq.com>

* [BugFix] normalize byte hash outputs in token database

- token_database.py: add a module-level helper to normalize hash outputs from vLLM hash functions into LMCache's int chunk-hash representation.

- Reuse the helper for both vLLM NONE_HASH initialization and token chunk hashing so sha256_cbor byte digests are folded consistently before entering the prefix hash chain.

Signed-off-by: GentleCold <gentlecold@qq.com>

* [CI] Fix isort in token database test

Signed-off-by: GentleCold <gentlecold@qq.com>
